data.title: HiPEAC 2026: Reclaiming European Digital Sovereignty
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

For decades, many believed the information technology (IT) sector was neutral. In the 1990s, code was often seen as existing beyond borders or politics. Earlier HiPEAC conferences focused almost exclusively on technical issues, but a keynote by Nextcloud CEO Frank Karlitschek at HiPEAC 2026 shifted attention to the political and existential dimensions of technology.

“In the 90s, IT was not political,” Karlitschek told the assembly of European engineers and researchers. At the time, he said, most politicians viewed it as something that “comes out of the wall socket, similar to electricity or water.” Today, however, that utility has been weaponized.

His presentation, called “Digital Sovereignty, or How We Can Take Back Control Over Our Digital Lives,” described how software now acts as an instrument of power. He argued that Europe’s reliance on American tech giants has become a serious risk.

Geography of the new oil

Karlitschek used the common phrase “data is the new oil,” but emphasized its capacity to generate conflict rather than its promise of economic opportunity. He compared today’s battles over digital infrastructure to the 20th century’s fights over oil.

“This is where our data is, and this is where our future is,” Karlitschek noted. “If data is the building block of the new economy… this is where our society and our companies and our future are controlled.”

This imbalance is now a genuine concern for European CIOs. A recent Gartner survey found that 61% of IT leaders in Western Europe plan to move workloads to local or regional providers to reduce geopolitical risk.

This concern was apparent at the last Gartner IT Symposium/Xpo in Barcelona. Former EU Commissioner Margrethe Vestager attended as people discussed the European Commission’s review of U.S. cloud contracts.

Triad of vulnerability

In his HiPEAC keynote, Karlitschek broke down the risks of this dependency into three main areas: availability, cost, and security. The most significant risk is availability, which is threatened by unstable legal rules governing data transfers across continents.

Europe’s rigorous General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) clashes fundamentally with U.S. surveillance laws, specifically the Clarifying Lawful Overseas Use of Data (CLOUD) Act, which compels American service providers to hand over data regardless of where it is physically stored.

Karlitschek explained that both the Safe Harbor agreement and the Privacy Shield failed. He predicted the current Data Privacy Framework (DPF) would also fail. 

A key rule in the DPF is that the U.S. Privacy and Civil Liberties Oversight Board (PCLOB) checks how things are run. But on Jan. 27, most board members were dismissed, leaving only one with a small legal team. A judge said firing two of the board members was unlawful.

“You can just declare that something is legal, which is obviously not legal,” Karlitschek said, referring to the repeated attempts to paper over the conflict between U.S. surveillance and EU privacy rights. “We can really wait for the day when giving data to U.S. cloud services becomes illegal again.”

Alongside legal risks, monopoly power is another problem. Many European organizations rely on Microsoft and Google, a dependency that has caused substantial price increases. Karlitschek noted that Microsoft increased prices by as much as 40% in early 2025, followed by an additional 33% rise later the same year.

“I don’t know a lot of businesses that can just raise their prices by 40%, and nothing happens,” Karlitschek observed. “Obviously, Microsoft can do this because it has a monopoly, so people cannot easily migrate away.”

The third risk, security, has moved from mere concern about spying to real industrial threats. As generative AI is integrated into cloud platforms, foreign companies could gain access to sensitive information stored in Europe. Karlitschek said. “You can totally assume that Boeing is interested in knowing what Airbus is doing, or that Tesla is interested in knowing what BMW is doing.”

Mechanisms of lock-in

The presentation described the technical and business barriers that big tech companies use to stop European organizations from switching to local options. Karlitschek talked about “interoperability roadblocks” and “vendor lock-in” tactics that keep the market closed.

He gave the example of Microsoft bundling Teams with Office 365. By offering Teams for free, Microsoft hurt the market for other standalone products before regulators could act. Even when regulators get involved, technical barriers still exist.

Karlitschek cited the difficulty of exporting chat histories from Teams and the lack of documentation for key APIs as deliberate strategies to hold customer data hostage. “We are happy to use them and give them all our information, but if you want to move away to some other solution, you cannot really get the data out anymore,” he said.

These barriers also affect mobile platforms. Karlitschek shared a story about Google and the Nextcloud Android app. Google revoked the app’s “All Files Access” permission, which is required for syncing, citing security. However, Google gave the same permission to its own Google Drive and Dropbox. Nextcloud only got the permission back after going to the press. “If you were like a two-person home developer… you cannot do what Google doesn’t like,” Karlitschek said.

Architecture of sovereignty

In response to these challenges, Karlitschek presented Nextcloud as more than just a product. He described it as a solution to the problems of centralized cloud systems. The company recently announced a €250 million investment in the “Sovereignty 2030” initiative and is focused on giving users complete local control.

Nextcloud’s main advantage is that it separates the software from the service provider. Unlike SaaS models, where the vendor keeps the data, Nextcloud runs on the customer’s own systems, whether on-site, in a local data center, or completely offline.

“We at Nextcloud build the software,” Karlitschek said. “We don’t have any data.” Because the software is 100% open source under the AGPLv3 license, the code is owned by thousands of contributors rather than a single corporation. This structure ensures that the license cannot be changed to a proprietary model in the future, providing long-term certainty for government and enterprise clients. “No one can change the license away from open source,” he noted.

To solve the problem of isolated self-hosted servers, Nextcloud uses a “federated” setup. This works like email, letting users on different servers communicate easily without a central authority.

“There is no central email server in the world… and this is exactly what we are doing here at Nextcloud,” Karlitschek said. This federation allows a university in Berlin to share sensitive research data directly with a facility in Paris, peer-to-peer, without the data ever transiting through a third-party hub in the U.S.

Technical evolution and AI

To address past performance complaints, Karlitschek shared details about the upcoming “Nextcloud Hub 26 Winter” release. The update has a new files backend built in Rust and Go. The Accelerated Direct Access (ADA) system lets users download files straight from storage, avoiding server slowdowns and allowing the platform to support millions of users.

The company is also responding to Microsoft Copilot by launching its own Nextcloud Assistant. To support digital sovereignty, the Assistant processes data locally on the user’s server. Its Context Chat uses Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) to answer questions using the user’s private documents, emails, and chats, without sending data to an outside AI model.

“This is a feature that might be a little bit scary if this comes from Google or Microsoft because they’re reading all your data,” Karlitschek noted. “But again, this happens completely locally on your machine.”

Reclaiming the future

With new regulations, higher costs, and better technology, Europe faces a key moment for digital sovereignty. As previously reported by EE Times, moving away from big tech providers is difficult, and Gartner analysts say it could take 10 years. Still, staying dependent on foreign infrastructure, with its risks of tariffs and political shutdowns, is becoming less acceptable.

Karlitschek believes the way forward requires two things: vigorous enforcement of EU rules, such as the Digital Markets Act, to break up monopolies, and a new focus on open-source development to create real alternatives.

“We can just give up and say… we don’t do IT anymore in Europe,” Karlitschek concluded. “Or we actually do something to go against these business practices […] to build good alternatives… so this is the only way forward.”

data.title: Canada Accelerates Quantum Aspirations
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

Canada’s quantum computing community has been busy.

On the heels of several high-profile Canadian quantum companies announcing their participation in the U.S. DARPA Quantum Benchmarking Initiative (QBI), the country’s federal government announced in December the creation of the Canadian Quantum Champions Program (CQCP).

Framed as a rival program to keep Canadian quantum firms and IP in Canada, the first phase of the CQCP is funding four companies—Xanadu, Nord Quantique, Photonic, and Anyon Systems—allotting up to about CDN$23 million (~US$16.7 million) each as non‑repayable, milestone-based contributions, for a total of up to CDN$92 million (~US$66.5 million).

Like DARPA QBI, the CQCP aims to support the development of scalable and useful fault-tolerant quantum computers. Hardware progress in CQCP is assessed by the Benchmarking Quantum Platforms initiative run by the National Research Council (NRC). Evan Solomon, Canada’s Minister of Artificial Intelligence and Digital Innovation, has signaled a shift toward defense-style procurement that echoes DARPA’s model.

Different quantum approaches

Montreal-based Anyon Systems has prioritized sturdy qubits through physics-informed engineering to address error challenges at the hardware level. By focusing on superconducting qubits optimized for lower error rates, the company is aiming for useful quantum computation with fewer than a million physical qubits rather than billions.

Nord Quantique, based in Sherbrooke, Quebec, takes a hardware-efficient approach to quantum error correction, using bosonic qubits encoded in superconducting cavities. The company’s qubits utilize multimode encoding in high-quality aluminum superconducting cavities coupled to transmon qubits for control to integrate error correction directly into individual qubits. This method avoids the need for thousands of physical qubits per logical qubit seen in conventional systems.

Rather than focusing on superconducting qubits, which require cryogenic temperatures, Toronto-based Xanadu employs a photonic approach, using light particles to encode and process qubits at room temperature to enable modular scalability and natural networking via optical fibers.

Similarly, Photonic claims its Entanglement First design enables any-to-any qubit connectivity across modules, racks, and data centers through its distributed, fault-tolerant quantum computing architecture based on silicon spin-photon qubits. In early 2025, the company introduced a low-overhead family of quantum low-density parity check codes, which can perform quantum computation and error correction at the same time while using materially fewer qubits than traditional surface code approaches.

Nord Quantique, Xanadu, and Photonic are all participating in the DARPA QBI, while both Xanadu and Photonic have attracted investment. Xanadu recently went public through a merger with U.S.-based Crane Harbor Acquisition, a special purpose acquisition company, while Photonic raised US$130 million in its latest financing round, attracting new investors including the Royal Bank of Canada and Canadian telecommunications firm Telus.

Global recognition

This flurry of quantum computing in Canada is not going unnoticed globally. A recent study by the European Patent Office and the Organization for Economic Co‑operation and Development shows that Canada ranks seventh worldwide, with 334 quantum-related international patent families (IPFs) from 2005 to 2024. D-Wave Systems, founded in Burnaby, B.C., and recently acquired Quantum Circuits, ranks 11th globally and sixth for quantum-computing patents.

The report noted that more than half—175 IPFs—relate to quantum computing, where the country ranks fourth. The country accounted for 3.2% of worldwide IPFs in the 2020-2024 timeframe, broadly stable compared to 3.3% in the 2015-2019 timeframe. It also has the highest degree of specialization in quantum technologies among leading countries, ahead of the U.K., Finland, the U.S., France, and the Netherlands.

Between 2014 and 2024, Canada accounted for 10.1% of global core quantum firms funded and 8.5% of global funding channeled to such firms—the second-largest share after the U.S.

More training and tools

Canadian quantum companies are getting a further boost with the introduction of the country’s first Quantum Computing Sandbox (QCS). The goal of the QCS is to accelerate the adoption of quantum computing technology by providing technical expertise and enabling access to state-of-the-art quantum computing platforms for academics, startups, and small and medium-sized enterprises.

The QCS is funded by FABrIC, a five-year, CDN$223 million (~US$161.5 million) project aimed at expanding Canada’s semiconductor ecosystem and fostering collaboration between industry, not-for-profits, academics, and government. FABrIC is managed by CMC Microsystems, a not-for-profit company that provides tools and training to Canada’s semiconductor industry.

The QCS will financially support direct access to quantum computing cloud services offered by global quantum computing providers and provide technical expertise from CMC’s dedicated team of quantum application scientists.

In a webinar to introduce the QCS, Luke Govia, manager of quantum technologies at CMC, said FABrIC membership is required to apply to the sandbox, which allows companies to apply for funding challenge calls. “You have access to the IP registry and repository, and there are many other ecosystem opportunities to help drive collaboration across Canada,” he said.

A key objective of the QCS is to accelerate the adoption of quantum computing technology within Canada, Govia said. “It’s not sufficient just to provide access to the platforms, the quantum computers themselves, but also to provide the technical expertise to deliver performant usage of those platforms.”

QCS has negotiated access to several quantum computing providers, including IBM and Pasqal.

The QCS is only open to Canadian organizations, although multinational and international organizations are allowed to collaborate as collaborators, but they’re not allowed to be the lead applicant, Govia said.

data.title: Indian Chip Design Services Provider Mirafra Tapes Out 22-nm SoC
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

Mirafra Technologies, a Bengaluru, India-based semiconductor design services company, has developed and taped out a 22-nm SoC called Ramanujan. The project is part of an internal effort to provide full end-to-end silicon delivery capability, rather than an attempt to enter the merchant chip market.

In an exclusive conversation with EE Times, Alok Kuchlous, co-founder and CEO of Mirafra, said the chip is intended as a demonstration vehicle to show that an Indian services firm can take responsibility for an entire SoC program—from architecture definition through physical design, software bring-up, and lab validation.

Chip architecture and design

Ramanujan is built around a dual network-on-chip (NoC) architecture that separates application processing from secure boot and system control functions, while allowing AXI-based (Advanced eXtensible Interface) communication between the two domains. The SoC integrates a single-core Arm Cortex-A55 application processor alongside a separate InCore RISC-V Azurite core for system and secure functions. The SoC supports external memory through SDRAM, Flash, and HyperRAM interfaces, along with on-chip SRAM and standard DMA-based data movement.

Security and system management functions are implemented on a dedicated system and secure NoC. This includes the Azurite system manager from InCore Semiconductors, which handles secure boot and early system control before the application processor starts.

The secure domain includes secure boot support, on-chip secure memory, hardware security logic, and always-available system peripherals. The chip also integrates CoreSight-based debug and test infrastructure, clock and power management blocks, and a mix of high-speed and low-speed peripherals, including UART, SPI, I²C, GPIO, and multimedia interfaces for display and camera connectivity.

The chip has been taped out and is being manufactured at TSMC. Board design for the demonstration platform, including a daughterboard, was also carried out internally. The remaining milestone is silicon bring-up and lab validation once wafers return.

Where third-party IP was unavailable or uneconomical, Mirafra developed blocks in-house, including camera and display controllers, inter-processor communication logic, and custom integration logic. The design was validated through FPGA prototyping. A full software stack, including Linux and multimedia components, was brought up on an FPGA implementation of the SoC and demonstrated publicly at the VLSI Design Conference 2026. The RTL used on the FPGA is the same as that sent for fabrication.

Customization would still require re-verification and a new physical design pass, but Kuchlous estimated the effort would be roughly half that of a clean-sheet design. “The architecture is modular, using standard interconnects and interfaces to reduce redesign scope,” he said.

The current chip is not positioned for industrial control or high-end AI workloads. It lacks analog interfaces, power drivers, and integrated image signal processing. While small neural networks could be supported, the design is not intended for advanced AI or harsh industrial environments.

From design services to product development

For the past two decades, Mirafra’s engagements largely involved subsystem ownership or limited design scopes. Ramanujan was conceived as a way to internalize the full flow, processes, and program management required to deliver a production-ready SoC.

Kuchlous said the move reflects a belief that Indian design services firms have reached a level of maturity where they can take ownership of complete chips, rather than executing narrow partitions of customer designs.

“This chip design is primarily targeted at building the capability to do an end-to-end project,” Kuchlous said. “We can take it to silicon and ship it along with the software.”

Kuchlous said he views this as particularly relevant for the Indian ecosystem, where government initiatives such as the Design-Linked Incentive (DLI) and Production-Linked Incentive (PLI) schemes are encouraging fabless startups and system companies to develop custom silicon. Many of these companies, he argued, lack the internal capability to integrate a complete SoC, manage physical design, bring up silicon, and validate it in the lab.

Kuchlous described the 22-nm process node as mainstream, stable, and appropriate for industrial and IoT applications. He said he does not expect Indian customers to target leading-edge nodes due to cost and risk considerations. The company intends to use the Ramanujan design as a reference platform that can be customized into derivative chips for original equipment manufacturers or fabless startups.

“We were not trying to sell this chip as a standalone product or compete with existing customers,” Kuchlous said. “If that had been the goal, we would have architected it differently. We are positioning it as a general-purpose IoT platform rather than a differentiated product.”

Measuring commercial success

The company expects commercial success to come primarily from winning turnkey ASIC or SoC integration projects, where customers provide specifications or core IP, and Mirafra delivers GDS or test silicon. High-volume consumer chip programs are considered possible but secondary.

In some cases, Mirafra may consider hybrid commercial models, such as partial upfront payment combined with royalties, particularly for smaller customers. However, its role remains that of a design partner rather than a fabless product company.

On pricing, Mirafra positions itself between international turnkey ASIC providers and Indian design houses. Kuchlous said international firms typically charge significantly higher rates for full-chip ownership, while most Indian firms lack equivalent end-to-end capability.

“Our differentiation is primarily on price against international players and capability against Indian players,” he said.

The design includes scan chains and memory built-in self-test to support high test coverage. Yield learning and lifecycle responsibilities would be defined contractually with customers, depending on engagement models.

Whether Ramanujan remains a one-off effort or evolves into a broader roadmap will depend on customer interest. Kuchlous said the team may consider future revisions at different nodes or with enhanced interfaces if demand emerges, but no roadmap has been committed.

Mirafra remains self-funded and has strengthened its sales presence in the U.S. to pursue larger, higher-ownership engagements. The company employs nearly 2,000 people, including around 1,500 engineers across design and software, all internal. Its main development centers are in Bengaluru and Hyderabad, with additional offices across India and teams in North America and Europe. While most of its revenue currently comes from North America and Europe, the company expects domestic engagement to grow as Indian fabless and system companies scale.

data.title: Renesas MCUs Now Accessible Via MIKROE In Multi-Year Deal
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

A multi-year development tool support agreement announced today between MIKROE and Renesas will provide developers with free access to 500 of Renesas’ most utilized microcontrollers (MCUs) and all new MCUs from the moment of launch.

MIKROE is known for its vast library of about 2,000 Click development boards, which are proven and tested to make them available for developers, its Necto Studio integrated development environment (IDE), and most importantly, its live remote debug farms called Planet Debug (see “Develop boards remotely, in real time with new hardware-as-a-service”), which enable designers to develop and debug embedded systems remotely without investing in hardware. Designers can reserve time on a remote Planet Debug station configured to their requirements in advance, then develop and debug their own applications code remotely through the Necto Studio IDE without having to source the hardware, wait for it to arrive, and plug it all together.

The agreement with Renesas commits MIKROE to providing development tools for 500 of Renesas’ most widely used MCUs, plus upcoming new releases. The Planet Debug remote board farm has also been established for Renesas MCUs.

In its announcement, MIKROE said tool support for the Renesas MCUs will be provided via both its Necto Studio IDE and Click board compact add-on boards to enable developers to rapidly provide proof-of-concept, then prototype, and code new embedded projects. The Click boards are fully compatible with the open source mikroBUS socket and can be used on any host system supporting the mikroBUS standard. They come with the mikroSDK open-source libraries, offering flexibility for evaluation and customization.

In an interview with EE Times, Neb Matic, MIKROE’s CEO, emphasized why this new partnership is a big deal for the company. “Silicon vendors are very sensitive about development tools. So Renesas choosing to work with us externally is a very important step. It shows trust in what we’ve built over many years,” he said. “We spent more than 10 years preparing this ecosystem. Only now do we finally have everything in place to offer this kind of service to a silicon vendor.”

He noted the importance of the development tools as well: “Development tools are a bridge between silicon vendors and customers. If the bridge isn’t good enough, it’s very hard for them to connect.”

Matic said that the new deal is another, very important revenue stream for MIKROE, on top of its current revenue streams which include selling Click boards and offering services. “This is a multi-year partnership,” he said. He didn’t rule out potential deals with other silicon vendors.

You can watch the full EE Times interview below:

In the announcement released today, MIKROE said it has been rolling out its Planet Debug remote board farms in Europe and the Americas. The company also plans to promote jointly with Renesas the hardware-as-a-service platform through extensive webinars and other programs. Designers using Renesas devices can reserve, at no cost, time on the remote board farm configured to their requirements.

This enables them to develop and debug their own applications code remotely through the Necto Studio IDE without having to source the hardware, wait for it to arrive, and then configure it. Planet Debug remote board farms are enabled by MIKROE’s CODEGRIP, which the company claims is the world’s first device enabling programming and debugging over Wi-Fi. Hence, with Planet Debug and Necto Studio IDE, designers can remotely view real images of real boards in real time, not through simulation.

In the press release, Mohammed Dogar, VP, Embedded Processing Marketing at Renesas, said, “This collaboration with MIKROE empowers our customers to begin development with our newest MCUs, immediately after they are announced. This gives them a significant head start and cost savings. By leveraging 24/7 access to boards on MIKROE’s Planet Debug remote board farms and choosing from thousands of functional code examples on the EmbeddedWiki embedded projects platform, Renesas customers gain direct access to code development resources that accelerate time to market.”

data.title: IonQ to Acquire Skywater and European Strategy
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

The global push for fault-tolerant quantum computing reached a new stage this month, marked by two very different strategies in the U.S. and Europe. In the U.S., IonQ announced plans to buy SkyWater Technology for about $1.8 billion to bring its supply chain in-house.

Focus in Europe is distributed, with a public-sector-driven approach, highlighted by the roadmap shared at the HiPEAC 2026 conference in Kraków, Poland. This shows a split in the industry: The U.S. is moving toward vertical integration and local manufacturing, while Europe is emphasizing academic partnerships, telecom infrastructure, and the integration of high-performance computing networks.

Vertical integration as an accelerator

On Jan. 26, IonQ and SkyWater Technology agreed that IonQ would buy the Bloomington, Minnesota-based semiconductor foundry. The deal, valued at $35.00 per share in cash and stock, aims to make IonQ the first fully vertically integrated quantum platform company.

IonQ’s main reason for the acquisition is to help scale up its technology. Trapped-ion quantum computers, which use electrically charged atoms (ions) as the basic units of information, rely on micro-electromechanical systems (MEMS)—tiny machines built onto semiconductor chips—to control individual ions. By bringing SkyWater’s manufacturing in-house, IonQ hopes to reduce the long wait times associated with using outside suppliers.

Niccolo de Masi, IonQ’s chairman and CEO, described the move as a strategic necessity for controlling the company’s destiny. “This transformational acquisition enables IonQ to materially accelerate its quantum computing roadmap and secure its fully scalable supply chain domestically,” de Masi said. He noted that the deal ensures “secure, U.S.-based design, packaging, and chip fabrication,” allowing the company to benefit from integration across quantum computing, networking, and sensing applications.

The main benefit of this deal is speed. In semiconductors, each design cycle can take months. By owning the foundry, IonQ expects to cut down wafer development times and move from serial to parallel prototyping.

IonQ expects these improvements to allow functional testing of its 200,000-qubit quantum processing units by 2028. These chips should support about 8,000 high-quality logical qubits, which is seen as key to solving real-world problems. The development of IonQ’s 2-million-qubit chip could also be sped up by as much as a year.

Thomas Sonderman, CEO of SkyWater Technology, emphasized that the merger aligns manufacturing precision with quantum innovation. “Joining forces with IonQ will accelerate multiple engineering pathways for next-generation quantum chips, delivering speed, precision, and scale,” Sonderman stated. The acquisition also secures IonQ’s status as a partner to the U.S. defense sector, as SkyWater operates a DMEA-accredited Category 1A Trusted Foundry, essential for handling classified and mission-critical workloads.

European model of federation and infrastructure

While IonQ is focusing on vertical integration, Europe is building a continent-wide network. This approach was a main topic at the HiPEAC 2026 conference in Kraków, where Krzysztof Kurowski, technical director at the Poznań Supercomputing and Networking Center (PSNC), discussed the challenges and opportunities of integrating quantum systems into existing computing systems.

The European strategy, led by the EuroHPC Joint Undertaking, does not focus on consolidating chip manufacturing under a single company. Instead, it centers on the “EuroQHPC-Integration” project, which aims to connect six quantum computers in Poland, the Czech Republic, France, Italy, Spain, and Germany into a single supercomputing networking block. 

“Quantum computers are a little bit more complicated…you probably cannot quite easily [snap them together like] Lego boxes and connect with existing HPC systems,” Kurowski observed. He argued that the immediate hurdle for the industry is not just qubit count, but the “software-hardware handshake,” specifically the latency involved in connecting classical supercomputers with quantum accelerators.

Europe’s approach relies heavily on leveraging its strong telecommunications sector and public infrastructure rather than hyperscale corporate investment. Kurowski noted the absence of U.S.-style tech giants in the European market. 

“We don’t have here in Europe big hyperscalers…but we have a pretty strong telco sector,” Kurowski said, highlighting that telecommunications companies are driving investment in quantum communication and quantum key distribution (QKD) networks.

Engineering hybrid systems

The differences between the two regions are both technical and organizational. IonQ’s plan relies on solving manufacturing challenges, such as producing complex electrode structures and silicon interposers (devices that allow electronic signals to pass between microchips) to move ions without overheating. 

SkyWater’s work in “More-than-Moore” technologies, which go beyond traditional silicon technology and include advances like through-silicon vias, which are vertical electrical connections passing through a silicon wafer, and advanced packaging, helps address these hardware issues.

In contrast, Europe is focusing on middleware and system architecture. Kurowski described the “PIAST-Q” project in Poznań, which connects an AQT trapped-ion system with traditional high-performance computing resources. The aim is to build a “system of systems” in which users can run jobs using quantum processors for certain tasks, such as combinatorial optimization or quantum machine learning, while classical CPUs handle most of the work.

This focus on integration has led to building large physical networks. Kurowski pointed out that PSNC has deployed over 1,700 kilometers of QKD channels across Poland; QKD uses quantum physics to securely share encryption keys. This may be the largest network of its kind in Europe.

This infrastructure is designed to support distributed quantum computing, in which qubits at different locations can be linked via optical connections. “We would like to take advantage of having a dedicated fiber channel between Poznań and Warsaw…to imagine having qubits entangled remotely,” Kurowski explained.

Implications for the industry

IonQ’s purchase of SkyWater puts pressure on other hardware companies to show they can manufacture at scale. By having its own supply chain, IonQ reduces the risks posed by global supply disruptions and capacity constraints that often affect the semiconductor industry.

The deal allows SkyWater to continue serving external customers as a merchant supplier, creating a unique hybrid revenue model that helps fund IonQ’s R&D.

On the other hand, the European model reduces technology risk by supporting a variety of approaches. By funding different types of quantum systems, such as superconducting circuits, trapped ions, and neutral atoms, Europe ensures its scientists can use a wide range of platforms.

However, this approach comes with the challenge of standardizing interfaces across different hardware vendors. Kurowski stressed the need for common application programming interfaces—standardized ways in which different software or hardware components interact—such as the Quantum Device Management Interface—to avoid fragmentation.

Looking ahead to 2030, the industry faces a choice between two scaling strategies. The U.S. model, as shown by the IonQ-SkyWater deal, is betting that controlling both the atomic and silicon components will lead to faster fault tolerance.

The European model, discussed at HiPEAC 2026, bets that quantum computing’s value will come from smoothly integrating these machines into the current global high-performance computing and telecommunications infrastructure.

Both strategies acknowledge that the time for lab experiments is ending, Kurowski said, pointing out that the industry is entering a phase in which reliability and dedicated infrastructure will shape the economy’s future.

data.title: HiPEAC 2026: Don’t Get Lazy with AI Optimization
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

Every year, just after CES in Las Vegas, Nev., EE Times travels across the Atlantic Ocean to the annual HiPEAC conference in Europe, which we think is one of the key European forums for experts in computer architecture, programming models, compilers, and operating systems for general-purpose, embedded, and cyber-physical systems.

While the focus might be very much on presenting the latest academic research (this year, there are three keynotes, 33 workshops, and three tutorial sessions), there is enough industry involvement to make it a good place for researchers to network, university spinouts to showcase their technologies, and companies like Arm, AMD, ASML, Google, and Huawei, among others, to recruit talent.

As Koen De Bosschere, the coordinator of the HiPEAC project European, as well as the head of the Department of Electronics and Information Systems at Ghent University, puts it, “The HiPEAC conference acts as a bridge across the computing stack, amplifying the impact of research and nurturing future computing leaders.”

The conference rotates locations around Europe, and this year, it is being hosted in Krakow, Poland. On day one, the keynote talk held by Michaela Blott of AMD focused on enabling efficient AI through silicon diversity, model optimization, agile AI stacks, and other innovative approaches. In the video interview below, you can hear what she had to say, as well as catch our thoughts on Blott’s opening keynote, plus some of the other sessions we attended during the day.

One of the key messages we took away from Blott’s opening talk was that there was a huge element of laziness in the community and industry as far as AI efficiency is concerned. It’s vital, she said, to pay attention to optimization at all levels to tackle some of the big challenges in AI becoming more efficient. “New algorithms are needed to bring AI efficiency in line with human performance and provide sustainable scaling,” Blott said. “Current methods are just too lazy.”

Blott’s message to the community was: “Don’t stop optimizing. Explore and co-design architectures with new algorithms, and in tandem with this, design better AI algorithms with better scaling properties.”

Technology meets society: cutting through the humanoid robotics hype

The great thing about listening to researchers who’ve actually done some real-world experiments with the technologies we talk about is that they inject a dose of reality. That was the case as we attended the “Technology Meets Society” track at HiPEAC 2026.

EE Times spoke to Micol Spitale, assistant professor at the Department of Electronics, Information, and Bioengineering at the Politecnico di Milano, Italy, who gave a talk on “socially intelligent robots” as part of this track, in which she explained that all the discussion about humanoid robotics is hyped up way too much. The reality is that there’s still a long way to go to meet the expectations that have been talked about for humanoid robotics.

In her work, she carried out research on groups of both children and adults in separate experiments to explore how support robots can be used to help promote human well-being.

In the following video interview, you’ll be able to understand more about her work and the potential for human-robot interaction to bring about technology for good:

PolyMage Labs, automatic compilers, and Tenstorrent partnership

Earlier this month, Indian startup PolyMage Labs announced that its automatic compiler for AI hardware was chosen by Tenstorrent for its AI platforms to improve software support for advanced AI hardware. PolyMage Labs said the partnership addresses a major challenge in the AI hardware industry: the lack of a mature and seamless software ecosystem, which significantly hinders the adoption of several AI accelerator platforms.

Uday Bondhugula, founder and CTO of Polymage Labs and a professor of computer science at the Indian Institute of Science in Bangalore, told EE Times more about the Tenstorrent partnership, as well as how the company is addressing current industry challenges to enable automated just-in-time compilation to support multiple AI frameworks. data.title: MosChip Completes 28-nm SoC for India’s Space Center
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

MosChip Technologies has completed the first silicon bring-up of a custom 28-nm SoC for the Space Applications Centre (SAC) in India under an end-to-end turnkey ASIC program.

The program covered design implementation, packaging, and post-silicon validation by MosChip. Engineering samples have been delivered and validated, allowing SAC to move into the next phase of productization.

SAC operates under the Indian Space Research Organization (ISRO) and is responsible for the design and development of satellite payloads and space-born systems. Its work spans earth observation, communication, navigation, and scientific missions for national and international users.

Following tape-out, MosChip carried out substrate design for a 10-layer FC-CBGA package and completed assembly and validation of engineering samples on automated test equipment. The silicon was verified against specifications, confirming functional correctness.

In an interview with EE Times, Swamy Irrinki, EVP of Worldwide Sales and Marketing at MosChip, said the turnkey execution model allowed MosChip to take ownership across multiple stages of the silicon and system lifecycle. The company positions itself as both a silicon company and an engineering services provider, depending on what the customer needs at a given point in the product cycle.

“Customers may want us to work on just one part of the product, or they may want us to take ownership of the full journey,” Irrinki said. “That could mean supporting a chip company or helping an OEM build a custom ASIC for its end product.”

Global business and domestic partnerships

MosChip works across telecommunications, networking, consumer electronics, high-performance computing, and storage. The company’s global footprint grew out of its early years in India. It began with a focus on product development for local needs, then expanded to serve customers in North America, Europe, and pan-Asia.

Today, most demand for custom silicon services still comes from outside India. North America accounts for the bulk of MosChip’s services business, followed by Europe and parts of Asia like Japan and South Korea. Irrinki said that India is beginning to generate its own custom silicon demand. He exemplified MosChip’s work with the Centre for Development of Advanced Computing (C-DAC), which is developing an indigenous high-performance computing processor, known as AUM, as part of India’s National Supercomputing Mission.

C-DAC has partnered with MosChip and Japan-based Socionext for the design and development of the processor, which is based on Arm Neoverse architecture and targeted for advanced process technology. The program is part of a broader effort to indigenize key components of India’s high-performance computing stack, including compute nodes, interconnects, and system software.

“The chip is defined by C-DAC, but designed by MosChip,” Irrinki said. “That includes the silicon, the boards, and the software.”

The processor is intended for use in supercomputing, weather monitoring, and research workloads at institutions like the Indian Institutes of Technology. It also supports national programs in aerospace and advanced research, including drug discovery.

In parallel, MosChip is developing its own product in select areas, including a smart energy meter IC for the Indian market. The chip is being developed under India’s Design Linked Incentive scheme, which provides financial support for semiconductor design projects. MosChip’s application for the smart meter IC was evaluated by C-DAC on behalf of the Indian Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology, with reimbursement linked to the completion of defined development milestones.

The company sees smart energy metering as a volume-driven opportunity. Industry estimates suggest the Indian smart meter market could reach around 60 million units by 2028, with global demand running into billions of units over the same period.

“These are niche areas where there is a clear local or government need,” Irrinki said. “Our primary goal remains servicing customers, not competing with them.”

IP ownership and execution risk

In most engagements, MosChip doesn’t own the IP. That typically remains with the customer.

“If we see a need for generic IP that can help multiple customers, we may develop it,” Irrinki said. “Otherwise, customers own their secret sauce.”

On the software side, MosChip focuses on reusable frameworks rather than licensable IP. These frameworks shorten development cycles and reduce time to market.

The biggest challenges are people and execution. Talent remains scarce, even in India, where competition for experienced engineers is intense. To address this, MosChip has built its own internal university to train engineers in the skills it needs. The aim is to create a steady talent pipeline rather than rely solely on lateral hiring.

Delivering right-first-time silicon is the other major challenge. Advanced nodes are costly, leaving little room for error. “We work closely with EDA, IP, and ecosystem partners to validate designs in silicon,” Irrinki said. “The goal is always first-pass success.”

MosChip’s recent engagements show a company working along a thin line between engineering services and silicon development. While it takes responsibility for design, packaging, validation, and system integration, it stops short of owning core IP or building merchant chips. It develops its own products selectively, mostly in areas driven by policy support or large-volume demand. In practice, MosChip positions itself as an execution partner that takes on technical complexity without competing with its customers in the market. This allows the company to deliver complete, working silicon while remaining rooted in a services-led business.

data.title: Hybrid Bonding Comes of Age Slowly and Collectively
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

When the IEEE Hybrid Bonding Symposium convened in Silicon Valley on Jan. 22 and 23, it did so with a noticeably broader and more industrially grounded agenda than its inaugural edition a year earlier. Hosted by SEMI International, the second annual event reflected a technology that is no longer confined to a narrow set of image sensor or memory applications but is steadily pushing toward mainstream heterogeneous integration—albeit with significant hurdles still to clear.

The 2026 program spanned materials science, surface chemistry, process integration, metrology, inspection, reliability, testing, and yield modeling. Taken together, the abstracts underscored how hybrid bonding has become a deeply interdisciplinary challenge. Papers explored sub-micron copper-to-copper bonding physics, dielectric surface preparation, chemical-mechanical planarization (CMP) control, stress and warpage, advanced X-ray and acoustic inspection, AI-assisted defect detection, and even digital-twin modeling of bond interfaces.

The breadth of topics signaled both progress and fragility: Hybrid bonding is advancing rapidly in pitch and performance, but its success increasingly depends on tight coordination across the entire process flow—from design through test and inspection—rather than on any single breakthrough.

Several abstracts also highlighted a shift in emphasis from proof-of-concept demonstrations toward manufacturability. While wafer-to-wafer bonding is now well established in high-volume production for image sensors and some memory devices, die-to-wafer bonding emerged as the central battleground for the next phase of scaling. Talks on particulate control, bond-front propagation, overlay accuracy, and CMP variability pointed to the less glamorous, but decisive, engineering work required to translate lab-scale success into profitable manufacturing.

Building a community around a fragile technology

At the center of this year’s symposium was Dongkai Shangguan, general chair of the event and an IEEE and IMAPS Fellow. Shangguan, who spent decades in semiconductor packaging and assembly before retiring from the industry, described hybrid bonding as the most promising interconnect technology available for achieving sub-micron pitch—yet also one of the most sensitive.

“For any new technology to really take root and commercialize, you need a community,” Shangguan told EE Times in an exclusive interview. “Hybrid bonding is extremely sensitive to particles, cleanliness, alignment, and process control. No single company or discipline can solve that alone.”

That conviction led Shangguan first to edit a comprehensive book on hybrid bonding, published in 2024, which brought together technical leaders across the ecosystem to document materials, processes, reliability, and applications. Contributors to that volume formed the backbone of the first Hybrid Bonding Symposium in 2025. This year’s event expanded beyond the book’s authors, drawing speakers from the U.S., Europe, and Asia to reflect a growing—and increasingly global—community.

The symposium’s evolution mirrors the technology’s maturity curve, according to Shangguan. Early discussions focused on feasibility and physics. Now, the conversation is shifting toward yield, throughput, and application breadth. “It’s becoming more mature and it’s being adopted in more application areas,” he said. “The key question is what additional breakthroughs we need to make those applications viable at scale.”

Testing and yield at sub-micron pitch

One of the clearest illustrations of hybrid bonding’s growing pains came from Erik Jan Marinissen, scientific director at imec, whose presentation focused on the implications of hybrid bonding for testing.

As pitches shrink well below 10 µm—and in imec’s research environment toward hundreds of nanometers—traditional assumptions about test access begin to break down. “From a test perspective, this technology is under development and not mature at all,” Marinissen told EE Times in an exclusive interview. The absence of any realistic roadmap for probing dense hybrid-bond arrays below ~25 µm pitch means that manufacturers must rethink pre-bond testing strategies entirely.

Hybrid bonding also changes the dominant defect profile. While shorts have historically dominated micro-bump interconnects, Marinissen noted that hybrid bonding shifts the balance toward clustered open defects caused by particles trapped between bonding surfaces. “A single particle can create a stand-off height that leads to many open defects, sometimes across die boundaries,” he explained. Conventional interconnect “repair” techniques—essentially rerouting signals around faulty connections—become far less effective when defects are spatially correlated.

The result is a growing tension between the need for rigorous pre-bond testing and the practical difficulty of performing it at fine pitch. Dedicated sacrificial probe pads may offer a workaround, but at the cost of additional area and process complexity. For stacked devices like high bandwidth memory (HBM) or logic-on-logic architectures, Marinissen sees the expansion of testing—from die level to chiplet level to full stack—as one of the most underestimated challenges ahead.

When copper physics takes over

While Marinissen highlighted system-level implications, Joke De Messemaeker, R&D group leader at imec, drilled into the physics that begins to dominate once hybrid bonding moves into the sub-micron regime.

At larger pitches, electrical contact between copper pads after bonding is largely driven by elastic expansion during annealing. Below about one micrometer, that mechanism effectively disappears. “Elastic expansion drops to around one nanometer or below, which is no longer reliable,” De Messemaeker told EE Times in an exclusive interview. Instead, surface-diffusion-driven “bulge-out” becomes the primary mechanism enabling Cu-Cu contact.

That shift has profound consequences for process integration. Surface diffusion is highly sensitive to copper cleanliness, microstructure, and local stress gradients, dramatically tightening requirements on surface preparation and CMP. De Messemaeker emphasized that understanding the bonding mechanism itself is now essential for yield engineering. “Once you understand what drives bulge-out, you can identify the knobs—pad design, grain structure, anneal conditions—that actually matter for controlling yield.”

Her work also points to a paradox of scaling: While bulge-out relaxes some CMP tolerances compared with relying solely on elastic expansion, it introduces new sources of variability tied to copper crystallography. Managing that variability, she said, will require deliberate microstructure engineering rather than simply tighter process control.

From ‘making it work’ to ‘making it profitable’

If imec’s perspective reflected the front edge of scaling, Vikas Dubey, senior scientist at Fraunhofer ENAS, framed hybrid bonding’s challenges in terms of industrialization.

“This year’s symposium shows that we are moving from ‘making it work’ to ‘making it profitable,’” Dubey told EE Times in an exclusive interview. While wafer-to-wafer bonding is already a mature high-volume process, die-to-wafer bonding exposes a series of manufacturing gaps that laboratories rarely face. Chief among them is cleanliness—not just within a single fab, but across an extended supply chain where dies may be processed, diced, transported, and bonded at different facilities.

Dubey also pointed to CMP as the single largest source of yield variability in high-volume manufacturing. Unlike alignment errors, which are immediately visible, CMP drift subtly alters surface nanotopography over time as pads and slurries wear. “If the dielectric is too high, copper won’t connect. If copper is too high, you get voids,” he said. Without robust post-CMP metrology and rework strategies, small variations can cascade into large yield losses.

Looking further ahead, Dubey argued that cost and equipment maturity are not the decisive barriers to broader adoption. Instead, the limiting factor may be design ecosystem readiness. “For hybrid bonding to become mainstream, we need standardized PDKs and EDA flows so that a typical fabless company can design for it without needing a PhD in tribology,” he said.

High yield, high throughput, more applications

For Shangguan, these disparate technical threads reinforce a single message: Hybrid bonding’s future depends as much on coordination as on innovation. Over the next five years, he expects symposium discussions to shift decisively toward process control strategies that deliver both high yield and high throughput—two goals that are often in tension at sub-micron pitch.

“Right now, you often slow down the process to achieve the alignment accuracy you need,” he said. “Hopefully, as the technology matures, we can have high yield, high throughput, and more applications.”

Those applications are already expanding beyond image sensors and HBM into logic-on-logic stacks, micro-LEDs, and even experimental approaches to thermal management using bonded micro-channel structures. Whether hybrid bonding ultimately fulfills that promise will depend on how effectively the community Shangguan is trying to build can translate deep physics insight into robust, repeatable manufacturing flows. If this year’s IEEE Hybrid Bonding Symposium offered a snapshot of where the technology stands today, it also made one thing clear: Hybrid bonding is no longer a niche curiosity—but neither is it a solved problem. Its path forward runs through collective learning, shared standards, and a willingness to confront the unglamorous realities of yield, test, and throughput head-on.

data.title: NAND Flash’s Reversal of Fortune Amid the AI Boom
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

The memory industry, long known for its hyper-cyclical business, is once more at a crossroads. It all began with an AI boom a few years ago, which made high-bandwidth memory (HBM) a device of choice alongside AI accelerators for training models. HBM, a specialized form of DRAM, offered far better profit margins than NAND flash, whose price drops and shrinking margins made large memory suppliers like Samsung and SK Hynix cautious about expanding NAND manufacturing capacity.

Moreover, on the technology side, as NAND surpassed the 200-layer mark, each new generation required advanced manufacturing equipment and massive capital investment. The most advanced NAND devices currently have more than 330 layers. So, while large memory vendors began redirecting capital investments toward HBM production, NAND capacity was no longer a strategic focus by 2023.

Both Samsung and SK Hynix turned their focus toward DRAM-centric HBM devices, offering much higher profitability, rather than building new NAND production lines. According to Omdia, Samsung lowered its NAND wafer output from 4.9 million in 2024 to 4.68 million in 2025. Likewise, SK Hynix’s NAND wafer output fell from around 1.9 million in 2024 to 1.7 million in 2025.

Samsung and SK Hynix haven’t yet announced any NAND flash capacity expansions. And these two companies command more than 60% of the NAND flash market.

However, in retrospect, NAND flash was also crucial in the AI scheme of things. For instance, shifting the strategic focus from training to inference workloads led to steady demand for NAND flash. Consequently, demand for NAND flash is surging as solid-state disks (SSDs) are now widely used for inference workloads in AI data centers.

Take the case of Kioxia, currently in partnership with Nvidia; the Japanese NAND flash maker plans to launch SSDs nearly 100 times faster in 2027. These SSDs, tailored for AI servers, are intended to replace HBM as a memory pair to Nvidia’s GPUs. According to Nikkei, Kioxia anticipates that by 2029, nearly half of its NAND flash will be used in AI applications.

Where Kioxia and Sandisk stand

While Samsung and SK Hynix are stuck in the HBM conundrum, what about Kioxia and Sandisk, which are not into the DRAM business and solely produce flash memory chips? Well, that’s another story, but not entirely immune to NAND supply challenges.

Start with Kioxia, which spun off as Toshiba’s memory business in 2017. It’s now past its turbulent days, thanks to a robust demand for NAND flash in AI inference workloads. Since its inception, Kioxia has been struggling due to corporate hiccups and brutal commoditization of NAND flash devices. But then an unprecedented demand for SSDs built around NAND flash led to a reversal of fortune.

The managing director of Kioxia’s memory business unit, Shunsuke Nakato, startled the market by disclosing that his company has sold out its entire NAND flash production volume for 2026. Another surprise: The company is starting the production of BICS10 NAND memory ahead of schedule in 2026. BICS10—a 332-layer 3D NAND array aiming to facilitate high-capacity storage solutions for AI and hyperscalers—was originally planned for production in the second half of 2027.

Sandisk, which spun out Western Digital in 2025, is also riding high due to continued NAND shortages and sharp price hikes of high-capacity SSDs. Like Kioxia, a prolonged NAND shortage could encourage Sandisk to increase its NAND output eventually.

Reversal of fortune

Memory super cycles are a routine affair in the semiconductor industry, but this time around, AI is rewriting the rules of the memory playbook. Nvidia chief Jensen Huang echoed this sentiment at CES 2026 when he called storage a “completely unserved market.”

The NAND flash market experienced brutal commoditization during the early 2020s, and in 2024, prices fell to a record low of 50%. Then came the AI boom, and NAND makers began moving away from consumer markets for smartphones, tablets, and laptops to a new class of AI servers that offered much healthier margins.

However, by then, the NAND flash industry had already entered a conservative production mode, which eventually thrust NAND underdogs such as Kioxia and Sandisk to the fore while leaving memory heavyweights such as Samsung and SK Hynix in a bind.

AI, in the true spirit of disruption, has turned the memory industry upside down on both the DRAM and flash frontiers. But while DRAM-based HBM devices have remained in the limelight for the past couple of years, the surging demand for NAND-based SSDs in AI clusters is now showing ripple effects.

There is a broad consensus that the structural imbalance in storage—encompassing both DRAM and NAND flash—will likely persist through 2026 and possibly into 2027. How NAND flash makers respond to this scarcity in AI and consumer markets will be a story to watch in 2026.

data.title: Prophesee Names New CEO as Fresh Funds Target Growth
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

Just before Christmas, French event-based vision firm Prophesee announced a major leadership change, with co-founder and long-time CEO Luca Verre stepping down and industry veteran Jean Ferré taking the helm. Ferré said his arrival coincides with renewed commitment from core investors, including iBionext, 360 Capital, Aramco, and Bosch Ventures. He also pointed to new backing from deep-tech fund Critical Path Ventures, describing this as “only the beginning.”

“Critical Path Ventures is joining our set of established [investors], and they asked me to come on board,” he told EE Times. “We have more new investors who should be joining. Given the operational priorities and market direction of the company, all parties involved decided new leadership was appropriate for this next chapter of Prophesee.”

“We thank Luca for his contributions to Prophesee and wish him well in his future endeavors,” he added.

An engineer by training and describing himself as an entrepreneur in AI, Ferré has founded AI startups and built a broad career across consultancy, aerospace, and big tech. “I’ve also worked in strategy consulting around the world [including] Boston Consulting Group, run air traffic management systems at Thales, and built products at Microsoft,” he said.

Against this backdrop, Ferré asked: “The thing is, how come Prophesee with so many patents, such mature technology, use cases proven in many research papers, and industry-grade sensors demonstrated with Sony and STMicroelectronics is not already as mainstream as it should be from a business perspective?” And this, of course, he intends to address.

Earlier days

Prophesee was founded as Chronocam in 2014 to exploit event-based image sensors that mimic the human eye by detecting changes in a scene pixel-by-pixel rather than capturing full frames. Rebranded in 2018, the company went on to raise some €126 million (about $147 million) and forge industry partnerships, including work with Qualcomm to optimize its Metavision sensors for smartphones and packaging its sensors in AMD Vision AI starter kits.

But despite the tech progress and initial commercial success, Prophesee filed for insolvency in October 2024. At the time, Luca Verre, then-CEO, wrote in a statement from the company: “The company has entered a judicial recovery procedure at the end of 2024 due to a delay in our fundraising process, which we are now in the final stages of completing with both existing and new investors.”

Just a little more than one year on, Prophesee says its financial issues are resolved, and with Ferré as CEO and investment forthcoming, the company has firmly set its sights on commercialization, targeting a “refined, product-centric, customer-oriented strategy.”

“We have the sensor, defined use cases, and the full-stack demonstration, [including] machine learning models to software integration in platforms such as Raspberry Pi,” Ferré said. “What probably [has been] missing is the scale of the business and demonstration of value.”

“Our technology is fantastic, but the way to make money with it…probably needed a bit of tuning, so this is what we’re doing,” he added.

Prophesee already has its tech in AR/VR, IoT, advanced driver assistance systems, consumer electronics, drone detection, and real-time industrial monitoring, but will “sharpen its near-term focus” on defense, security, aerospace, and industrial automation sectors.

“There are multiple companies [here] that are aggressive and convincing in terms of what they want to do and how much value they see,” Ferré said. “I’ve been on the phone with one of our integrators for Electronic Supervision System cameras, and they said, ‘we’ve never sold so many evaluation kits in so many industries—drones, manufacturing’. There’s traction [here]…this is huge.”

“This is a different approach from saying, ‘let’s empower the self-driving car of the future, let’s demonstrate [our technology] works, and then wait for the demand in a supply chain that still has to be put in place,’” he added.

Easy integration

While Prophesee’s sensors are already MIPI compliant, the company aims to make integration easier for developers. “We’re going further [than MIPI compliance],” Ferré said. “We want this to be much more turnkey; we want to make it very simple.”

Prophesee will also look at how it is managing its data pipeline so users can better analyze and potentially merge event-based data with frame-based cameras. Further ahead, Ferré sees an opportunity for Prophesee to support AI developers building frontier models. “We won’t build the models—that’s too expensive—but we could provide the datasets, sensors, and applications to the engineers building the models,” he said. “That’s down the road but is definitely on my mind and on our agenda. I think Prophesee has a unique capacity to absorb granular data and can help to capture more of the reality of the physical world.”

If recent event camera market forecasts hold true—Lucintel, for one, predicts a buoyant 14% plus CAGR over the next seven years—then Ferré’s plans for Prophesee and the latest funds have arrived at the right time. Ferré also noted solid growth forecast in industrial camera markets for advanced vision systems, highlighting: “The volumes are huge, and a lot of these [systems] can be replaced or complemented with event-based cameras.”

For the CEO, focusing on new commercial opportunities and growing the company is what Prophesee will be about from here on in. When asked about acquisition potential—given the recent SynSense-iniVation merger, and myriad market heavyweights—he replied: “We’re talking to very powerful players. They are not looking to buy us.”

“We have a great standalone story, and the important thing to do now, is bring impact,” he said. “Evidence indicates this market [will be] huge, and we are totally ready to scale: We have the sensors, we have the use cases, we have the playbook.”
